rationale_for_baseline_tier: Viral pharyngitis is normally self-care, but severe odynophagia preventing all oral intake in a patient living alone creates a genuine dehydration risk, warranting prompt (24–48h) review and a low threshold for ED if oral rehydration fails (T2). Peritonsillar abscess is screened negative. If analgesia restores drinking, effective tier is lower; if it fails, escalate.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Trismus (can't open mouth), muffled hot-potato voice, one-sided throat swelling, uvular deviation, or drooling
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests peritonsillar abscess/deep-neck infection needing urgent ENT assessment and possible drainage.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't open your mouth fully, your voice becomes muffled, you notice one-sided swelling in the throat, or you start drooling — go to the Emergency Department that day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to drink despite analgesia, not passing urine >8h, marked dizziness/weakness, or muffled voice with breathing difficulty
escalation_edges.rationale: Failed oral rehydration or significant dehydration may need IV fluids and assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you still can't drink after taking pain relief, you stop passing urine, or you feel very weak/dizzy — go to the Emergency Department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone; no one to monitor intake or deterioration
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower threshold for review/ED; arrange a definite check-in and provide explicit dehydration red flags.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Take pain relief (paracetamol/ibuprofen) about 30 minutes before drinking, then sip cold fluids and ice chips regularly. If you manage to keep drinking, you can care for this at home. Arrange a review within 1–2 days.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Prompt GP/telehealth review within 24–48h; ED if you can't rehydrate or abscess features develop
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If you cannot drink at all or feel very unwell overnight, attend an after-hours service or ED.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Acute viral pharyngitis (low Centor), abscess screen negative, at risk of dehydration (living alone, poor intake); analgesia-before-fluids plan; review hydration in 1–2 days.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Pure self-care with no hydration plan or dehydration safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Patient living alone cannot drink, becomes progressively dehydrated overnight with no monitoring, and presents late.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine review only, missing an evolving peritonsillar abscess or failed rehydration.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling an ambulance for a viral sore throat that is drinking after analgesia — unnecessary emergency resource use.
